title: Bicycle Accidents
description: Cyclists are among the most vulnerable road users. When a bicycle is struck by a vehicle, the injuries can be severe. Knowing your rights, documenting the scene, and understanding how liability works are essential first steps after a bicycle accident.
metaTitle: Bicycle Accident Guide — Rights, Steps & Claims After a Crash
metaDescription: What to do after a bicycle accident in California or Arizona. Evidence checklist, immediate steps, and guidance on speaking with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Dooring Accidents
commonCauses.description: A parked car occupant opens a door into a cyclist's path, causing a sudden collision. Common in urban areas with bike lanes adjacent to parked cars.

commonCauses.title: Failure to Yield at Intersections
commonCauses.description: Drivers turning or pulling out of driveways may fail to yield to cyclists in the bike lane or roadway.

commonCauses.title: Rear-End Collisions
commonCauses.description: Drivers following too closely or distracted may strike cyclists from behind, often resulting in serious injuries.

commonCauses.title: Left-Cross Accidents
commonCauses.description: A driver turning left across the path of an oncoming cyclist — one of the most common causes of serious bicycle accidents.

commonCauses.title: Road Hazards
commonCauses.description: Potholes, debris, rail tracks, and uneven pavement can cause cyclists to lose control and crash, sometimes without vehicle involvement.

commonCauses.title: Distracted Driving
commonCauses.description: Drivers using phones or otherwise distracted are more likely to miss cyclists, especially at intersections and in bike lanes.

commonCauses.title: Driving Under the Influence
commonCauses.description: Impaired drivers have reduced ability to detect and respond to cyclists sharing the road.

immediateSteps.title: Call 911
immediateSteps.description: Request emergency services. A police report is essential documentation even if injuries seem minor.

immediateSteps.title: Stay at the scene
immediateSteps.description: Do not move your bicycle or allow the vehicle to be moved before photos are taken. If you are on the road, move to a safe location while waiting for police.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve your helmet and gear
immediateSteps.description: Your helmet, gloves, and clothing are physical evidence. Do not discard, wash, or repair them.

immediateSteps.title: Document the scene thoroughly
immediateSteps.description: Photograph all vehicles, your bicycle, damage, road conditions, the bike lane or road markings, and any visible injuries.

immediateSteps.title: Get the driver's information
immediateSteps.description: Name, license number, insurance company, and contact details. Note the vehicle make, model, and plate.

immediateSteps.title: Get witness information
immediateSteps.description: Names and contact details from anyone who saw the accident.

immediateSteps.title: Seek medical evaluation immediately
immediateSteps.description: Bicycle accidents can cause significant head and internal injuries. Accept ambulance transport if offered, or go to urgent care or an ER the same day.

immediateSteps.title: Report to your insurer
immediateSteps.description: Notify your insurer. If you have uninsured motorist coverage, it may apply. Keep all records organized.

items: Photos of the vehicle and your bicycle (before moving anything)
Photos of the road, bike lane, and any road defects
Photos of any visible injuries
Driver's license, insurance, and contact information
Police report number
Names and contact info of witnesses
Your bicycle (preserve for damage assessment)
Your helmet (do not discard — it shows impact severity)
Your clothing, gloves, and shoes (do not wash)
Photos of damage to your gear
Emergency room or urgent care records
All follow-up physician and specialist notes
Imaging reports (X-ray, MRI, CT scan)
Physical therapy records
Prescription records and ongoing symptom journal
All medical bills
Bicycle repair or replacement estimate
Lost wages documentation
Any accident-related out-of-pocket expenses
Dashcam or traffic camera footage
Surveillance video from nearby businesses
Any prior complaints about the road hazard (if applicable)

timelineRisks.risk: Helmet and gear may be discarded
timelineRisks.action: Preserve all riding gear as physical evidence. Store safely and do not clean or repair.
Traffic and business cameras typically overwrite within 24-72 hours. An attorney can send a preservation request immediately.
Report to your insurer promptly. Review your policy for uninsured motorist coverage that may cover bicycle accidents.
If a road defect maintained by a government agency caused or contributed to the accident, California and Arizona have shorter notice-of-claim deadlines.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: California generally allows 2 years from the date of accident for personal injury claims.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Arizona has the same 2-year personal injury statute of limitations. Contact an attorney well before this deadline.

insuranceIssues.issue: Personal auto UM/UIM coverage for cyclists
insuranceIssues.explanation: Many auto insurance policies extend uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage to policyholders even when they are on a bicycle. Review your policy carefully.
In some cases, your own homeowner or renter insurance may cover bicycle accident injuries or property damage. Check your policy.
Insurers may argue you were not following traffic laws, riding without a light at night, or otherwise contributing to the accident. Both states use pure comparative fault.
If a pothole or road defect contributed to your accident, a government entity may be partially liable. These claims have special procedures and shorter deadlines.

whenToGetLawyer: You sustained injuries requiring medical treatment
The driver was uninsured or fled the scene
A road defect or poorly maintained infrastructure contributed to the accident
Your injuries resulted in significant medical bills or lost wages
You received a quick settlement offer before understanding your full injuries
